coredata.dc:title: Hydrothermal green synthesis of magnetic Fe3O4-carbon dots by lemon and grape fruit extracts and as a photoluminescence sensor for detecting of E. coli bacteria
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Ahmadian-Fard-Fini, Shahla
Salavati-Niasari, Masoud
Ghanbari, Davood
coredata.dc:description: The aim of this work is preparing of a photoluminescence nanostructures for rapid detection of bacterial pathogens. Firstly, carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by grape fruit, lemon, turmeric extracts and hydrothermal method. Then Fe3O4 (magnetite) nanoparticles was achieved using these bio-compatible capping agents. Finally, magnetite-carbon dots were synthesized as a novel magnetic and photoluminescence nanocomposite. X-ray diffraction (XRD) confirms the crystallinity and phase of the products, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) investigate the morphology, shape and size of the magnetite, carbon dot and nanocomposites. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy shows the purity of the nanostructures. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption and photo-luminescence (PL) spectroscopy show suitable photo-luminescence under ultraviolet irradiation. Vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) shows super paramagnetic property of the product. Interestingly carbon dots were used as a non-toxic photoluminescence sensor for detecting of Escherichia coli (E. coli) bacteria. Results show quenching of photoluminescence of the CDs nanocomposite by increasing amount of E. coli bacteria.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nanostructures
Green synthesis
Photoluminescence
Bacteria

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of preparation of carbon quantum dot from lemon or grape fruit extracts.
XRD pattern of Fe3O4-carbon dot nanocomposite.
Carbon dots on the black tissue under UV light.
SEM image of Fe3O4 nanoparticles prepared in the presence of lemon extract.
SEM image of Fe3O4 nanoparticle synthesized in the presence of turmeric extract.
SEM images of carbon dot nanoparticle made in the presence of lemon extract.
SEM images of carbon dot nanoparticles prepared by Ethylene Diamine-Citric acid.
SEM images of carbon dot nanoparticle synthesized by Ethylene Diamine-Grapefruit.
SEM images of magnetite-carbon dot nanocomposite.
(a–d). TEM images of carbon dot nanoparticles.
(a–c). TEM images of magnetite-carbon dot nanocomposite by grape fruit extract.
TEM images of magnetite-carbon dot core-shell nanocomposite by (a) lemon extract (b) citric acid.
VSM curve of Fe3O4-carbon dot nanocomposite.
UV spectra of products at 180 °C in the presence of (a) lemon extract (b) grape fruit.
PL spectra of products at 180 °C in the presence of (a) lemon extract (b) citric acid.

head.ce:title: Hydrothermal green synthesis of magnetic Fe3O4-carbon dots by lemon and grape fruit extracts and as a photoluminescence sensor for detecting of E. coli bacteria

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology, University of Kashan, Kashan, P.O. Box 87317-51167, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Science, Arak University of Technology, Arak, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The aim of this work is preparing of a photoluminescence nanostructures for rapid detection of bacterial pathogens. Firstly, carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by grape fruit, lemon, turmeric extracts and hydrothermal method. Then Fe3O4 (magnetite) nanoparticles was achieved using these bio-compatible capping agents. Finally, magnetite-carbon dots were synthesized as a novel magnetic and photoluminescence nanocomposite. X-ray diffraction (XRD) confirms the crystallinity and phase of the products, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) investigate the morphology, shape and size of the magnetite, carbon dot and nanocomposites. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy shows the purity of the nanostructures. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption and photo-luminescence (PL) spectroscopy show suitable photo-luminescence under ultraviolet irradiation. Vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) shows super paramagnetic property of the product. Interestingly carbon dots were used as a non-toxic photoluminescence sensor for detecting of Escherichia coli (E. coli) bacteria. Results show quenching of photoluminescence of the CDs nanocomposite by increasing amount of E. coli bacteria.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted abundant of attention and turned into a new class of fluorescent nano-materials due to their superior instincts comprise optical and electronic properties, non-toxicity, abundant and inexpensive nature. CDs are a type of small carbon nanoparticles with size less than 10 nm and are related to zero dimensional quantum dots. CDs are usually considered as quasi zero dimension and fragments of graphene sheets [1–4]. Fluorescent semiconductor nanocrystals are used for serving as probes in cell imaging and other biomedical applications. There have been a number of experiments in the literature on carbon dots for fluorescence labeling to replacing of conventional semiconductors by CDs with a growing demand on high performance fluorescence sensors. More effective surface passivation has been correlated with brighter fluorescence emissions. Wide attention has been focused on bio-imaging and biomedicines properties of CDs specially in luminescence (as electron donors and acceptors for sensor and catalyst) because of high solubility, chemical resistance to photobleaching, availability, high emission quantum yields, size-tunable emission, narrow spectral bands and facile surface functionalization for a specific sensing without the optical blinking [2, 6–10].

Many procedures were reported for preparation of CDs for example arc discharge, ion beam radiation, oxidation, solvothermal and microwave methods. Bacterial metabolic tests are standard protocols for bacterial identification but usually this process takes time for identification of the bacteria that is not suitable in ill emergency conditions [3, 11–15]. Several ultra-sensitive detection methods based on nucleic acid amplification were introduced. These technologies need isolation of bacterial DNA, expensive equipment's for nucleic acid amplification, and preparation of enzyme reaction mix. The rapid detection of bacterial pathogens is very important for medical diagnostics, biosecurity, transportation and the prevention of global pandemics [16–21].

Escherichia coli (E. coli) is a Gram-negative, rod-shaped, coliform bacterium that is found in the intestine. Some of them can be responsible for food contamination. It is expelled into the environment within fecal matter. Some virulent strains can lead to gastroenteritis, urinary infections, meningitis and hemorrhagic colitis. Recently numerous budgets are spent to treat waste water of factory and drinking water. Every year about 7 million people die annually from the consequences of disasters and water-related diseases [22, 23].

In this work fluorescent carbon quantum dot were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal treatment in which lemon, grape fruit and turmeric extracts were used as natural material. Photo luminescence was quenched by increasing amount of E. coli bacteria that these outcomes introduce a novel nanocomposite for bio sensing application.

Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, FeCl2·6H2O, NaOH, distilled water and methanol were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Company. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded by Philips company using Ni-filtered CuKα radiation. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained using a MIRA3-TESCAN instrument. All the chemicals were used as received without further purifications. GC-2550TG (Teif Gostar Faraz Company, Iran) were used for all chemical analyses. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy and Ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) were obtained by PerkinElmer. Room temperature magnetic properties were investigated using an vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) device, (Meghnatis Kavir Kashan Co., Iran) in an applied magnetic field sweeping between ±10,000 Oe. Prior to taking SEM images, the samples were coated by a very thin layer of Pt (using a BAL-TEC SCD 005 sputter coater) to make the sample surface conductor and prevent charge accumulation, and obtaining a better contrast.

1 g of natural extract (turmeric, lemon, citric acid and grapefruit) was dispersed in 40 ml of deionized water, and it was stirred for 30 min. Ethylene diamine was also added to the solution. Then the dispersion was transferred into Teflon lined stainless autoclave at 180 °C for 6 h. Different parameters such as time, temperature, and ratio of extracts were studied. The final product was soluble in water.

0.01 mol of FeCl2 4H2O and 0.02 mol of iron nitrate nonahydrate were dissolved in 180 ml of water. 1 g of lemon, grape fruit or turmeric extracts was also added to the solution. 15 ml of NaOH (1 M) was added to the solution until reaching pH to the 11. Then the dispersion was transferred into Teflon lined stainless autoclave at 180 °C for 6 h, the brown-black precipitate was centrifuged and washed by distilled water for 2 times.

0.1 g of magnetite was dispersed in 40 ml of water and then 1 g of lemon (or grape fruit, turmeric) extract and ethylene diamine solution were added to the solution, and the dispersion was transferred into Teflon lined stainless autoclave at 180 °C for 6 h. The precipitate was washed by distilled water and ethanol.

Synthesis of carbon dot from lemon or grape fruit in the presence of ethylene diamine by applying hydrothermal procedure is schematically depicted in Fig. 1 . Fig. 2 illustrates XRD pattern of magnetite product. Pure cubic phase of inverse spinel (JCPDS No. 01-1111) can be observed in this pattern. XRD pattern of Fe3O4-carbon dot nanocomposite obtained at 180 °C for 6 h is depicted in Fig. 3 . Presence of magnetite in the carbon dot matrix was approved by XRD pattern. As we know carbon dot has amorphous structure, so there are some noises related to C-dot in comparison to pure Fe3O4.

For photoluminescence investigation CDs samples were added to a black tissue and were placed under ultraviolet irradiation. Typing word is not visible under ambient light; however, it can be appropriately seen under UV light (Fig. 4 ). The understanding of the origin of fluorescence in carbon nanoparticle is far from sufficient. For example, information on the microstructure and surface ligands remains unclear and details of the organic passivation is not sufficient to aid understanding of the surface states beneficial for light emission. Preparation of C-Dots include three main stages; carbonization, passivation, and surface functionalization [4, 6, 7]. Carbonization is the stage that precursors convert into its carbon residues via pyrolysis, strong dehydration, or destructive distillation. The chemical treatment of the surface (is named passivation), an insulating shell on the surface minimizes the impact of surface-defect, trap, and quenching from the surrounding, which lead to increase in fluorescence emission. In functionalization stage chemical or biological reactivity is added on the surface [4, 6, 7].

Scanning electron microscopy images of Fe3O4 nanoparticles prepared in the presence of lemon extract are shown in Fig. 5 that approve formation of mono-disperse nanoparticles, according to images average particle size is less than 30 nm. Fig. 6 shows SEM images of Fe3O4 nanoparticles synthesized in the presence of turmeric extract that confirm synthesis of mono-disperse nanoparticles and illustrate the mediocre size is about 50 nm. Fig. 7 illustrates SEM of carbon dots made by ethylene diamine-lemon extract at 180 °C for 6 h. Results approve the average particle size is around 50 nm.

SEM of carbon dots that were synthesized by ethylene diamine–citric acid at 180 °C for 6 h are depicted in Fig. 8 . As the scanning electron microscope confirm size of the carbon dots are less than 20 nm. Fig. 9 illustrates SEM of quantum dots were made by ethylene diamine-grape fruit extract at 180 °C for 6 h. These images also approve agglomerated product were produced but size of the carbon dots are less than 30 nm.

SEM of Fe3O4-carbon dots nanocomposite prepared by ethylene diamine-lemon extract at 180 °C for 6 h are shown in Fig. 10 , images illustrate formation of mono-disperse nanocomposites with average particle size less than 40 nm.

As we know SEM is not suitable for such a small nanoparticles and because of this reason TEM images were applied in the following. Fig. 11(a–d) illustrates transmission electron microscopy of graphene quantum dots prepared at 180 °C for 6 h. TEM images of the obtained product show that these CDs are uniform in size ranging around 20 nm in diameter, CDs stacking to form bigger particles. This happens because at higher temperature most of smallest particles stack and form larger particles with inhomogeneous sizes. Fig. 12(a–c) depicts TEM of Fe3O4‑carbon dots nanocomposite which were synthesized by grape fruit at 180 °C for 6 h. TEM images of the prepared sample show that these CDs are uniform and diameter size is about 20 nm [12–15].

By focusing in the transmission electron microscopy images we can find appropriate core-shell nanostructures. Fig. 13a and b shows TEM images of Fe3O4-carbon dot obtained by lemon extract and citric acid respectively which approve core-shell formation. Fe3O4 as a magnetic core was separated completely from carbon dot as a photo-luminescence shell.

Fig. 14 shows FT-IR spectrum of the prepared CDs, peaks at 400–600 are related to the FeO (metal-oxygen) bonds, peaks at 1547, 1656 are related to CO group and broad absorption at 3254 cm−1 is corresponding to OH respectively. Peak at 1047 and 1216 cm−1 are attributed to presence of CO group. Also absorption at 2935 cm−1 is related to CH bonds.

Room temperature magnetic properties of samples were studied using vibrating sample magnetometer instrument. Hysteresis loops of magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles and Fe3O4-carbon nanocomposite are shown in Figs. 15 and 16
respectively. The nanoparticle samples exhibit sufficient magnetizations which can simply be attracted by a magnet, making them appropriate for core of the sensor. The hysteresis loop also indicates that the as-synthesized nanoparticles show super paramagnetic behaviour with saturation magnetization of 65 emu/g and nearly 0 coercivity. The hysteresis of Fe3O4-carbon nanocomposite also shows super paramagnetic behaviour with saturation magnetization of 34 emu/g and nearly 5 coercivity.

Magnetic interaction between the nanoparticles surrounded by carbon dots was investigated. This interaction leads to an increase (from 0 Oe to 5 Oe) of nanocomposite coercivity in comparison to pure magnetite nanoparticles. Apparently, magnetic domains are pined by carbon dots and as a result higher magnetic field is needed for changing in magnetic domains of ferrite.

UV–Vis spectrum of carbon dots by lemon and grape fruit extract at 180 °C at 6 h are shown in Fig. 17a and b respectively. The carbon dots shows absorption bands around 350 nm which is attributed to π-π* transition of graphene quantum dots that confirm preparation of graphene from natural extracts.

Photo luminescence of samples at 180 °C by ethylene diamine–citric acid and by ethylene diamine–lemon extract are shown in Fig. 18 and interestingly results confirm the quenching of PL by increasing amount of E. coli bacteria. PL intensity around 460 and 520 nm were obtained with the excitation wavelength of 350 and 450 nm respectively. Citric acid show higher PL intensity in the higher wave length (520) while lemon extract shows lower PL intensity in the lower wave length (460 nm). In PL of CDs, two main reasons are proposed. Surface/edge state in CDs that explains when graphene is cutting along different directions, armchair and zigzag edges can be obtained. Edge types are more important key in electronic properties of CDs. Oxygen and amine on surface of CDs influence on optical band gap. For this reason, synthesis method and starting materials play important role on electronic properties of CDs. Second is attributed to quantum confinement in CDs. The origin of the PL of CDs has been assigned to several reasons such as size (quantum effect), defect, surface groups and passivation, and the recombination of electron–hole pairs localized within sp2 carbon embedded in a sp3 matrix [1–3].

Its optical absorption is related to π-plasmon transitions in the core of the dots, while the fluorescence emissions in the visible to near-IR are because of photogenerated electrons and holes trapped at various surface sites and their associated radiative recombination. CDs also have two-photon excitation cross sections in the near-IR, with the resulting fluorescence emissions comparable to those associated with the regular excitation. Carbon dots show high quantum yield, which make them a suitable candidate for fluorescent probes in fluorescence detection. Carbon dots show higher quantum yield and stability as they show fluorescence after six months [4, 6, 7].

Under the optimized operational conditions, fluorescence signals of different concentrations of CDs-labeled E. coli were detected. This results show that amount of unknown concentration of these bacteria at this range, can be measured as well. This quantum confinement converts CDs to non-zero band gap semiconductor that have PL on excitation. Addition of bacteria to CDs solution lead to surface functional group and as a result quenching is occurred [7, 21]. Quantitative detection of E. coli bacteria was obtained from Stern-Volmer relationship F0/F = 1 + KSV[C]; Where F0 and F are the PL intensities in absence and presence of E. coli, respectively, [C] is bacteria concentration and Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant. A standard curve (Fig. 18) was obtained in the concentration range from 0 to 9 × 107 colony-forming unit per milliliter [CFU/ml] also log[C] was used instead of [C] [7].
CHNO elemental analysis for presence confirmation of carbon dot on the Fe3O4 core was used (Table 1 ).

Carbon dots with high quantum yield were synthesized using lemon, grape fruit or turmeric extracts in the presence of ethylene diamine. These prepared carbon dots show suitable fluorescence stability under UV irradiation. The quantum dots were prepared by a facile method and its quantum yield was about 20%. A rapid and sensitive method for quantitative detection of E. coli bacteria using carbon dots as a novel fluorescent sensor was introduced. This proposed method was easy in operation with high sensitivity and specificity.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are grateful to the council of Iran National Science Foundation (INSF) and University of Kashan for supporting this work by Grant No (159271/8990).
